address.addrLine: The John Rylands Research Institute and Library

addrLine.ref: The John Rylands Research Institute and Library

msIdentifier.repository: The John Rylands Research Institute and Library

msContents.summary: A work on the game of chess by Yaḥyá ibn Ibrāhīm al-Ḥakīm.

author.persName: Abū Zakarīyā Yaḥyá ibn Ibrāhīm al-Ḥakīm
ابو زكريا يحيى بن ابراهيم الحكيم

msItem.title: Nuzhat arbāb al-ʿuqūl fī al-shiṭranj al-manqūl
نزهة أرباب العقول في الشطرنج المنقول
Delight of the Intelligent in the Known (Game of) Chess

note.p: Another treatise on chess composed by Ibn Abī Ḥajalah, Aḥmad ibn Yaḥyá is held at the John Rylands Library: Arabic MS 59.

Ibn Abī Ḥajalah (d. 776/1375), the author of Arabic MS 59, who died in 776 AH (1375 CE) mentions Abū Zakarīyā Yaḥya al-Ḥakīm's present treatise. Abū Zakarīya quotes the Ḳāmūs of Fīrūzābādi, who died in 817/1414.

The author does not appear to give a name to his book. On folio 2a however, there is a note assigning to the book the following title: كتاب النزهة في الشطرنج. On folio 1a (the old flyleaf now foliated) a more recent hand added a longer title: نزهة ارباب العقول في الشطرنج المنقول (Delight of the Intelligent in the Known (game of) Chess. This last title has been accepted by Nathaniel Bland in his description of present manuscript in his article on Chess (JRAS 1852).

After the usual sayings as to the excellence and antiquity of the game (with which Adam consoled himself for the loss of his son, cf. folio 3a), and how it should be played, come the six ṭabaḳāt (folio 17b), the values of the pieces (folio 18a), the kinds of games (folio 19a); from which point on to folio 39b, the text is reduced to a narrow column down the outer margins, the greater part of each page being devoted to diagrams of games drawn mostly from the works of العدلي (of unknown date; see Mingana for possible identifications). This section ends on folio 40a ( middle of line 5) with the formula ثم كتاب المنصويات.

Folios 40a-50a: selected verses referring to the game, introduced by the formula باب اذكر فيه ما وصلني من الشعر الذي يذكر فيه الشطرنج. قال ابو زيد ابن العامة من شعراء الاندلسي في الاستدعاء اليها (folio 40a, from the end of line 5).

Folio 51a: قال ابو العباس ابن جريح ومولدي سنة ثلاث وثلاثين خمسمائة وحدثني بما ذكوية عند كتابتيل هذا الكتاب في اخر سنة ثمان وتسعين وخمسمائة بمراكش حرسها الله تعالي وبها توفي ابو العباس ابن جريح في ذي القعدة في سنة ثلالين ستمائة.
Folio 51b begins with the rubric: ومن حواش الكلام علي اللعي.
Folios 52a-54a: verses, some illustrated by diagrams, of tricks for the chess board.

msItem.incipit: قال الفقيه الكاتب الاديب ابو زكريا يحيى بن ابراهيم الحكيم عفا الله عنه الحمد الله الذى بين الحلال والحرام ... وبعد فهذا كتاب وضع في الشطرنج من كتب شتى. قال الله تعالى الخ
msItem.explicit: تم اخرا يزيد نظرك. كمل الكلام والله الغفور الرحيم
msItem.colophon: وكان الفراغ من نسخه يوم الاربعاء رابع جمادى الآخرة سنة ٩١٢ والحمد الخ

supportDesc.foliation: Modern foliation in Western Arabic numerals in pencil.

condition.p: Water damage throughout, usage stains, smudged ink.
Substantial repairs on folio 1 with loss of text.

layoutDesc.layout: Text written in 1 to 2 columns. Number of written lines varies. Well rubricated.

handDesc.handNote: Naskh script, in black and red ink. Probably written by at least two hands (Hand A up to middle of folio 12a).

decoDesc.decoNote: Numerous chess diagram from folio 19b onwards.

provenance.p: Several notes in Arabic script in ink on the front paste-down (to be deciphered).
Note in Arabic in ink on folio 1a (to be deciphered).
According to the note on the front pastedown, the manuscript was in Damascus before arriving to England: Damascus No. 16.

Before 1806, the manuscript belonged(?) to George Cecil Renouard (1780-1867) classical and oriental scholar, who served as chaplain to the British Embassy at Constantinople 1804-1806, and Lord Almoner's Professor of Arabic at Cambridge University 1815-1821. In his collection, the manuscript was successively numbered 7 and 76; see note on flyleaf ia: "G.C.R. N.J. New Cat. No.76, Cat. 1840, No. 146". Futher note: "Nozhetu erbābi'l ukūl fi'sh shetrenji'l menkul. A Treasure on the Game of Chess, apparently of considerable antiquity. G.C.R." from Damascus No. 16.

Formerly in the collection of John Lee (formerly Fiott) (1783-1866), the antiquary and astronomer, a Fellow of St John's College Cambridge. Lee's bookplates on the front pastedown: 1) an oval globe azure on a chevron between three lozenges or and anchor erect with cable sable. The crest is a demi-horse rampant, charged on the shoulder with a fluer-de-lys sable, on a wreath of the colours. Beneath it is written: 'John Fiott, B.A. / St John's College. Cambridge / 1806.' ; 2) a shield of a coat of arms with two crests. The shield is in quarters, with first and fourth quarters azure, two bars or, over all a bend chequy gules and of the second, and the second and third quarters azure, on a chevron between three lozenges or an anchor erect with cable sable. The crests: a bear passant sable, muzzled, collared, and chained, and a demi-horse rampant, charged on the shoulder with a fleur-de-lys sable. Beneath the shield is the motto: 'Verum atque decens', and the name of the engraver: 'Mutlow Sc[ulpsit] York St[reet].' In Dr Lee's catalogue, the manuscript was numbered 146 - this number is written in pencil in the upper outer corner of the first flyleaf. Lee's oriental manuscript collection was catalogued by previous owner George Cecil Renouard.

The Persian scholar Bland, Nathaniel (1803–1865)Nathaniel Bland (1803–1865) borrowed the manuscript from Dr. Lee when preparing a paper on Persian Chess (see his article in the JRAS, 1852), but failed to returned it (see Murray's The History of Chess, p. 53-54). His bookplate on the back pastedown: Bland MSS no. 167.

Bland's oriental manuscripts were sold after his death through Quaritch, Bernard (1819–1899)Bernard Quaritch (1819–1899) in 1866 to Crawford, Alexander Crawford Lindsay, Earl of (1812–1880)Alexander Lindsay, 25th Earl of Crawford (1812–1880).
Formerly in the collection of Alexander Lindsay, 25th Earl of Crawford (1812–1880) Crawford, Alexander Crawford Lindsay, 1812-1880.
Purchased by Enriqueta Rylands (1843–1908) Rylands, Enriqueta, 1843–1908 in 1901 from James Ludovic Lindsay, 26th Earl of Crawford (1847–1913) Crawford, James Ludovic Lindsay, Earl of, 1847–1913 .

acquisition.p: Bequeathed by Enriqueta Rylands (1843–1908) in 1908 to The John Rylands Library.

recordHist.source: Catalogue information compiled by Abdullah al-Rashidi from Alphonse Mingana, Catalogue of the Arabic Manuscripts in the John Rylands Library, Manchester University Press, 1934, revised and expanded by Zsófia Buda.

availability.p: To book an in-person or online appointment to consult the manuscript, visit Using the Special Collections Reading Rooms. For any other enquiries please email uml.special-collections@manchester.ac.uk.

listBibl.bibl: Lee, John and G.C. Renouard. Oriental Manuscripts Purchased in Turkey and Now Preserved in the Library at Hartwell in Buckinghanshire. London: Richard Watts, 1831. Second edition 1840.
Bland, Nathaniel. "On the Persian Game of Chess." Journal of the Royal Asiatic Society 13 (1852): 1-70, especially from page 27.
A. Mingana, Catalogue of the Arabic Manuscripts in the John Rylands Library Manchester (Manchester: Manchester University Press, 1934), no. 766 [86].
Murray, H.J.R. A History of Chess (1913), pages 175-176.

bibl.ref: Kruk, Remke. "A Courtier's Chess Book: Ibn Abī Ḥaǧalah's Unmūd̮aǧ al-qitāl fī naql al-ʿawāl." In: The Sultan’s Anthologist - Ibn Abī Ḥaǧalah and His Work, ed. Nefeli Papoutsakis and Syrinx von Hees, pp. 159-178. Baden-Baden: Ergon Verlag, 2017.

listBibl.bibl: Filippoupoliti, Anastasia. "Spatializing the Private Collection: John Fiott Lee and Hartwell House." In Material Cultures, 1740-1920: the Meanings and Pleasures of Collecting, edited by John Potvin and Alla Myzelev (Farnham: Ashgate, 2009), 53-69.

revisionDesc.change: Julian Cook Marked up with persName elements free-format names related to provenance.
Julian Cook Assigned role(s) to every person having an id (Fihrist data cleanup project).
Abdullah al-Rashidi compiled record. Elizabeth Gow converted record to TEI.
